articles.title: Judge Leans Into The Dystopia Of 2026
articles.content:
**编辑说明：** 欢迎来到我们的每日栏目"每日趣味问答"！

**问题：** 哪位州高等法院的克雷格·A·卡尼斯法官最近维持了一项地方规定，允许在该州拥有财产的公司、有限责任公司、信托和合伙企业进行投票？

**提示：** 他承认了其中的科幻元素，写道："想象无脸大公司甚至像HAL那样控制一个小镇是令人恐惧的，这是科幻小说的素材，"但"信托、合伙企业、有限责任公司和公司被州法律明确认定为'人'"。

**答案见下页。**

本文首先发表于Above the Law。
articles.summary:
Ed. Note: Welcome to our daily feature Trivia Question of the Day!

Judge Craig A. Karsnitz of which state’s Superior Court recently upheld a local provision authorizing voting by in-state corporations, LLCs, trusts, and partnerships owning property?

Hint: He recognized the scifi of it all, writing, “Visions of faceless large corporations or even HAL controlling a small town are frightening and the stuff of science fiction,” but “trusts, partnerships, limited liability companies, and corporations are expressly recognized as ‘persons’” by state law.

See the answer on the next page.

The post Judge Leans Into The Dystopia Of 2026 appeared first on Above the Law.

articles.title: Now Is Not The Time
articles.content:
最近，《纽约时报》发表了一篇评论文章，一位在顶级律所（具体是WilmerHale——也是ATL“脊椎指数”列出的仅有的四家律所之一）工作的第五年律师放弃了法律执业。他离开的原因是看到了令人不安的政治干预问题。

我理解那些处境相似的人为什么会说“我要走了”，但我也认为现在就下这个结论还为时过早，尤其是在WilmerHale。如果他是从那些屈服让步的众多律所之一离开，我或许更能理解，但他偏偏从仅有的四家在政治干预面前有勇气说“不”的顶级律所之一离开。

当今做律师比以往任何时候都更加艰难。这位作者30岁才进入法学院。回顾我早期的执业岁月，我们那时候其实容易得多，尽管当时并不这么认为。但如今，经济压力、业务开发责任、学生贷款偿还以及人工智能的冲击同时袭来，坚持下去变得异常困难。即便是顶级律所的高薪也不一定能弥补这些。

我们姑且承认执业环境已经不同了。在那些“恐龙时代”，年轻律师可以期待获得一些庭审经验，可以期待辩论一些动议，就较小的事务为客户提供咨询。如今，合伙人们需要继续填满自己的份额，所以分享工作甚至委派工作都变得更加困难。人工智能不会使分享变得更容易，因为它可以非常快速高效地完成各种任务，尽管存在准确性和幻觉问题。当蛋糕变得更小时该如何分配？一块蛋糕能切出多少片？这取决于你如何切，又有多少人愿意接受更小的份额？你告诉我。

离开的律师对执业的许多方面感到幻灭。律所倡导的某些方式让他困扰：如何呈现事实，如何构建论点，以及如何组织支持客户应该胜诉的理由案件。事实和法律被有目的地以对客户有利的方式撰写，这是诉讼的本质。每个人都有权获得代理，而在顶级律所，初级律师通常无权做出这样的决定。最终，律师费驱动着法律业务的运作。

他也认为“法律体系已经崩溃，急需修复”。这位作者进入法学院和开始执业都比较晚，他对顶级律所的模式感到不满意，这与“安慰受苦的人、折磨安逸的人”恰恰相反。他看到律所帮助富有的客户变得更加富有。他说WilmerHale是一个很好的工作场所，但令他难以接受的是“法律执业的民主责任”与“高度利润驱动的产业”之间存在的令人不安的对比。

对他来说，这是一种他无法调和的矛盾。他是否太早跳下了顶级律所的仓鼠轮？这不是我能说的，但我一想到所有那些沉没的成本——时间和金钱——怀揣着很高的希望，如今希望破灭，就感到心疼。

对于那些下个月要参加律师资格考试的人，正在等待考试结果的人，或者刚刚开始执业的人，不要让你们现在在联邦政府中看到的景象打消你们对法律职业的追求。在地方和州一级有很多机会。那些想要代表个人对抗冷漠企业实体的人有大量机会，那些希望通过在各种非营利组织工作来做出改变的人也有机会，这些组织不仅代表人们，还倡导法律必要的变革。

“恐龙时代”的氛围要融洽得多。虽然我不主张每周五天都回到办公室工作，但我喜欢走在走廊里，向某个领域的专家请教：“你怎么看这个？最好的处理方式是什么？有没有什么特别的法律需要查阅？”这比谷歌搜索要好，也是发展友谊和知识的最佳方式。很少有同事会拒绝我的请求。

这一代新律师需要所有的智慧火焰和对法治的承诺。弄清楚你真正想作为律师实现的目标。找到一个能激发和激励你的执业领域。听从一个经历过多次尝试和挫折最终找到最适合自己的路的人（也就是我）的建议。温斯顿·丘吉尔（如果你不知道他是谁，谷歌一下）据说曾说过：“当你正在经历地狱时，继续前进。”所以，继续前进。

Jill Switzer是加州律师协会的活跃成员，已经四十多年了。她记得在更加友善、温情的时代执业。她的法律职业生涯多样化，包括在地方检察官办公室担任副检察官、独立执业和几份高级内部法律职务。现在她从事全职调解工作，这让她有机会看到恐龙、千禧一代和介于两者之间的人互动——并不总是文明的。你可以通过电子邮件oldladylawyer@gmail.com联系她。

《现在不是时候》这篇文章首次发表在Above the Law。
articles.summary:
In a recent New York Times op-ed, a fifth-year associate at a Biglaw firm (WilmerHale to be specific — and one of only the four that ATL listed on its “Spine Index”) threw in the law practice towel. He left because of what he saw as a troubling overlay of political interference.  

I understand why people, similarly situated, would say “I’m outta here,” but I also think that it’s too soon to make that call, especially at WilmerHale. Perhaps I would better understand if the departure was from one of the many firms that caved, rather than from one of only four Biglaw firms that has had the courage to say “no” to 47.

It’s harder than ever to be a lawyer of whatever vintage these days. The author started law school at the ripe old age of 30. Looking back on my earliest lawyering years, we had it easy, although we didn’t think so. But today, when economic pressures, business development responsibilities, student loan repayments, and AI collide, it’s hard sometimes to stay the course. Even Biglaw monies cannot necessarily compensate.

Let’s stipulate that the practice is different now. In dinosaur times, young lawyers could expect to get some trial experience, could expect to argue some motions, and counsel clients on smaller matters. Today, partners need/want to continue to fill their own trough, so sharing work, let alone delegating it, is harder. AI will not make sharing any easier, given how quickly and efficiently it can do various tasks, regardless of accuracy and hallucinations. How to divide the pie when pieces are smaller? How many slices can you get out of a layer cake? Does it depend upon how you slice it? And how many are willing to take smaller pieces? You tell me.

The departing associate was disillusioned about a number of things in practice. Parts of the firm’s advocacy troubled him: how you present the facts, how you structure the argument(s) and cases that support the reasons why the client should prevail. The facts and law are purposefully written in the client’s favor; that’s the nature of advocacy. Everyone deserves representation, and in a Biglaw firm associates do not usually get to make that call. At the end of the day, attorney fees drive the business of lawyering.

He also believes that the “legal system is broken and is in dire need of repair.” The author, a relative latecomer to law school and then to practice, is not happy about what he sees as the Biglaw paradigm, the reverse of the “comfort the afflicted and afflict the comfortable.” He saw the firm help rich clients get even richer. He says that Wilmer Hale was a wonderful place to work, but it rankled him that a disturbing contrast existed between the “democratic responsibilities of lawyering” and the “highly profit-driven industry.” 

To him, it was a conflict that he couldn’t resolve. Did he jump off the Biglaw hamster wheel too soon? Not for me to say, but I wince when I think about all the sunk costs (time and money) spent with high hopes and now dashed dreams.

For those of you taking the bar next month, waiting for bar results, or in your early years of practice, don’t let what you see now in the federal government dissuade you from your legal careers. There are opportunities for lawyers at the local and state levels. those who want to represent people against faceless corporate entities, opportunities for those who want to make a difference by working for various nonprofits that represent people but also advocate for necessary changes in the law.

It was a much more collegial atmosphere in dinosaur days. While I am not advocating a RTW five days a week, I liked walking down the hall and asking for help from a subject matter expert: “What do you think about this? How best to handle it? Any particular law to look at?” Better than googling and the best way to develop friendship and knowledge. Few, if any, colleagues refused my request.

This new generation of lawyers needs all the intellectual firepower and commitment to the rule of law that it can muster. Figure out what you really want to accomplish as a lawyer. Find a practice area that stimulates and motivates you. Take it from someone (aka me) who had a number of different fits and starts as a lawyer before finally latching on to what was best for me. Winston Churchill (Google him if you don’t know who he was) reportedly said, “When you are going through hell, keep going.” So, keep going.

Jill Switzer has been an active member of the State Bar of California for over 40 years. She remembers practicing law in a kinder, gentler time. She’s had a diverse legal career, including stints as a deputy district attorney, a solo practice, and several senior in-house gigs. She now mediates full-time, which gives her the opportunity to see dinosaurs, millennials, and those in-between interact — it’s not always civil. You can reach her by email at oldladylawyer@gmail.com.

The post Now Is Not The Time appeared first on Above the Law.
